subject: language
stem: Which dialogue sentence is correct?
explanation: Commas and quotation marks correctly divide the dialogue tag.

choices.label: A
choices.body: "Please wait," said Theo "for the next bus".
choices.misconception: Misplaced punctuation around dialogue.

choices.label: B
choices.body: "Please wait," said Theo, "for the next bus."

choices.label: C
choices.body: "Please wait" said Theo "for the next bus."
choices.misconception: Misplaced punctuation around dialogue.

choices.label: D
choices.body: Please wait," said Theo, "for the next bus."
choices.misconception: Misplaced punctuation around dialogue.

subject: language
stem: Which sentence uses commas correctly?
explanation: A comma follows the introductory phrase after lunch.

choices.label: A
choices.body: After, lunch we visited the science lab.
choices.misconception: Misplaced the comma after an introductory phrase.

choices.label: B
choices.body: After lunch we visited, the science lab.
choices.misconception: Misplaced the comma after an introductory phrase.

choices.label: C
choices.body: After lunch, we visited the science lab.

choices.label: D
choices.body: After lunch we, visited the science lab.
choices.misconception: Misplaced the comma after an introductory phrase.

subject: language
stem: Which word is spelled correctly?
explanation: Because is the correct spelling.

choices.label: A
choices.misconception: Did not recognize the high-frequency spelling.

choices.label: B
choices.misconception: Did not recognize the high-frequency spelling.

choices.label: C
choices.misconception: Did not recognize the high-frequency spelling.

subject: language
stem: Which sentence has no spelling errors?
explanation: Their, favorite, and place are spelled correctly.

choices.label: A
choices.body: Their favorite place is the library.

choices.label: B
choices.body: Thier favorite place is the library.
choices.misconception: Missed a common spelling error.

choices.label: C
choices.body: Their favrite place is the library.
choices.misconception: Missed a common spelling error.

choices.label: D
choices.body: Their favorite plase is the library.
choices.misconception: Missed a common spelling error.

subject: language
stem: Which word is spelled correctly?
explanation: Usually has two l letters.

choices.label: A
choices.misconception: Chose a common misspelling of a frequent word.

choices.label: C
choices.misconception: Chose a common misspelling of a frequent word.

choices.label: D
choices.misconception: Chose a common misspelling of a frequent word.

subject: language
stem: Which sentence is spelled correctly?
explanation: Believe, answer, and correct are all spelled correctly.

choices.label: A
choices.body: I believe the anser is correct.
choices.misconception: Failed to spot the misspelled high-frequency word.

choices.label: B
choices.body: I believe the answer is corect.
choices.misconception: Failed to spot the misspelled high-frequency word.

choices.label: C
choices.body: I believe the answer is correct.

choices.label: D
choices.body: I beleive the answer is correct.
choices.misconception: Failed to spot the misspelled high-frequency word.

subject: language
stem: Which combines the sentences best? Nia drew a map. Zoe labeled it.
explanation: Use comma and and to join two complete sentences.

choices.label: A
choices.body: Nia drew a map Zoe labeled it.
choices.misconception: Created a run-on or fragment while combining.

choices.label: B
choices.body: Nia drew a map, Zoe labeled it.
choices.misconception: Created a run-on or fragment while combining.

choices.label: C
choices.body: Nia drew a map and. Zoe labeled it.
choices.misconception: Created a run-on or fragment while combining.

choices.label: D
choices.body: Nia drew a map, and Zoe labeled it.

subject: language
stem: Which is the best compound sentence?
explanation: But joins two complete contrasting ideas.

choices.label: A
choices.body: Aarav practiced the song, but he still felt nervous.

choices.label: B
choices.body: Aarav practiced the song but still nervous.
choices.misconception: Combined sentences without a proper conjunction.

choices.label: C
choices.body: Aarav practiced the song, he still felt nervous.
choices.misconception: Combined sentences without a proper conjunction.

choices.label: D
choices.body: But Aarav practiced the song, he still felt nervous.
choices.misconception: Combined sentences without a proper conjunction.

subject: language
stem: Choose the best way to combine: The rain stopped. We went outside.
explanation: So shows the result and joins the complete thoughts.

choices.label: A
choices.body: The rain stopped so. We went outside.
choices.misconception: Made a comma splice or fragment.

choices.label: B
choices.body: The rain stopped, so we went outside.

choices.label: C
choices.body: The rain stopped we went outside.
choices.misconception: Made a comma splice or fragment.

choices.label: D
choices.body: The rain stopped, we went outside.
choices.misconception: Made a comma splice or fragment.

subject: language
stem: Which sentence combines ideas without changing meaning?
explanation: And correctly shows both actions happened.

choices.label: A
choices.body: Imani studied the chart, Theo wrote the summary.
choices.misconception: Used punctuation or conjunction that changes the relationship.

choices.label: B
choices.body: Imani studied the chart, because Theo wrote the summary.
choices.misconception: Used punctuation or conjunction that changes the relationship.

choices.label: C
choices.body: Imani studied the chart, and Theo wrote the summary.

choices.label: D
choices.body: Imani studied the chart Theo wrote the summary.
choices.misconception: Used punctuation or conjunction that changes the relationship.

subject: language
stem: Which is a complete sentence?
explanation: A complete sentence has a subject and complete predicate.

choices.label: A
choices.misconception: Chose a fragment.

choices.label: B
choices.misconception: Chose a fragment.

choices.label: C
choices.misconception: Chose a fragment.

subject: language
stem: Which revision fixes the run-on?
explanation: Use a comma and conjunction to join complete thoughts.

choices.label: A
choices.body: We missed the turn, so we checked the map.

choices.label: B
choices.body: We missed the turn we checked the map.
choices.misconception: Left a run-on or comma splice.

choices.label: C
choices.body: We missed the turn, we checked the map.
choices.misconception: Left a run-on or comma splice.

choices.label: D
choices.body: We missed, the turn we checked the map.
choices.misconception: Left a run-on or comma splice.

subject: language
stem: Which group of words is a fragment?
explanation: After the game ended is incomplete by itself.

choices.label: A
choices.misconception: Did not notice the dependent word after.

choices.label: C
choices.misconception: Did not notice the dependent word after.

choices.label: D
choices.misconception: Did not notice the dependent word after.

subject: language
stem: Which revision makes the fragment complete?
explanation: Add an independent clause to complete the idea.

choices.label: A
choices.misconception: Left a dependent clause as a sentence.

choices.label: B
choices.misconception: Left a dependent clause as a sentence.

choices.label: C
choices.body: Because the trail was muddy, we turned back.

choices.label: D
choices.misconception: Left a dependent clause as a sentence.

subject: language
stem: Which sentence fixes the comma splice?
explanation: Use a conjunction after the comma to join two sentences.

choices.label: A
choices.body: The speaker finished, the audience clapped.
choices.misconception: Used a comma splice or run-on.

choices.label: B
choices.body: The speaker finished the audience clapped.
choices.misconception: Used a comma splice or run-on.

choices.label: C
choices.body: The speaker, finished the audience clapped.
choices.misconception: Used a comma splice or run-on.

choices.label: D
choices.body: The speaker finished, and the audience clapped.

subject: language
stem: Which is a complete simple sentence?
explanation: The sentence has a subject and predicate.

choices.label: B
choices.misconception: Chose an incomplete sentence.

choices.label: C
choices.misconception: Chose an incomplete sentence.

choices.label: D
choices.misconception: Chose an incomplete sentence.

subject: language
stem: Which is a complete compound sentence?
explanation: Both sides of the conjunction are complete thoughts.

choices.label: A
choices.body: Maya read the poem Ava drew.
choices.misconception: Chose a sentence missing a subject or predicate.

choices.label: B
choices.body: Maya read the poem, and Ava drew a picture.

choices.label: C
choices.body: Maya read the poem and Ava.
choices.misconception: Chose a sentence missing a subject or predicate.

choices.label: D
choices.body: Maya read, and drew a picture.
choices.misconception: Chose a sentence missing a subject or predicate.

subject: language
stem: Which sentence is complete and correct?
explanation: Both clauses are complete and agreement is correct.

choices.label: A
choices.body: The students quiet, but the hallway noisy.
choices.misconception: Missed agreement or sentence completeness.

choices.label: B
choices.misconception: Missed agreement or sentence completeness.

choices.label: C
choices.body: The students were quiet, but the hallway was noisy.

choices.label: D
choices.body: The students was quiet, but the hallway were noisy.
choices.misconception: Missed agreement or sentence completeness.

subject: language
stem: Which revision creates a complete sentence?
explanation: The revision adds a predicate to the subject.

choices.label: A
choices.misconception: Mistook a phrase for a complete sentence.

choices.label: B
choices.body: On the table belongs to Theo.
choices.misconception: Mistook a phrase for a complete sentence.

choices.label: C
choices.misconception: Mistook a phrase for a complete sentence.

choices.label: D
choices.body: The book on the table belongs to Theo.

subject: language
stem: Which sentence is complete and avoids a run-on?
explanation: A comma and conjunction correctly join the two complete thoughts.

choices.label: A
choices.body: The hikers stopped, and the guide checked the map.

choices.label: B
choices.body: The hikers stopped the guide checked the map.
choices.misconception: Created a run-on or comma splice.

choices.label: C
choices.body: The hikers stopped, the guide checked the map.
choices.misconception: Created a run-on or comma splice.

choices.label: D
choices.body: The hikers, stopped and the guide, checked the map.
choices.misconception: Created a run-on or comma splice.

subject: language
stem: Which sentence would make the best introduction?
explanation: An introduction states the main idea.

choices.label: A
choices.misconception: Chose a detail or conclusion instead of an introduction.

choices.label: B
choices.body: School gardens help students learn in many ways.

choices.label: C
choices.body: That is why gardens are useful.
choices.misconception: Chose a detail or conclusion instead of an introduction.

choices.label: D
choices.misconception: Chose a detail or conclusion instead of an introduction.

subject: language
stem: Which transition best begins a final paragraph?
explanation: In conclusion signals the ending paragraph.

choices.label: A
choices.misconception: Chose a transition for a detail or sequence step.

choices.label: B
choices.misconception: Chose a transition for a detail or sequence step.

choices.label: D
choices.misconception: Chose a transition for a detail or sequence step.

subject: language
stem: Which sentence belongs in a conclusion?
explanation: A conclusion restates the main idea and wraps up reasons.

choices.label: A
choices.body: One reason is that students need computers.
choices.misconception: Chose a supporting detail instead of a closing idea.

choices.label: B
choices.body: For example, Priya used the printer.
choices.misconception: Chose a supporting detail instead of a closing idea.

choices.label: C
choices.misconception: Chose a supporting detail instead of a closing idea.

choices.label: D
choices.body: These reasons show why the library should stay open later.

subject: language
stem: Which order is best for an essay?
explanation: A clear essay begins, develops, and ends.

choices.label: B
choices.misconception: Put essay parts in an illogical order.

choices.label: C
choices.misconception: Put essay parts in an illogical order.

choices.label: D
choices.misconception: Put essay parts in an illogical order.

subject: language
stem: Which detail best supports: Exercise helps students focus?
explanation: The detail directly supports focus after exercise.

choices.label: A
choices.misconception: Picked an unrelated detail.

choices.label: B
choices.body: After running, our class listened carefully.

choices.label: C
choices.misconception: Picked an unrelated detail.

choices.label: D
choices.misconception: Picked an unrelated detail.

subject: language
stem: Which sentence adds the most specific detail?
explanation: Specific details name what, where, and how long.

choices.label: A
choices.misconception: Chose a vague detail.

choices.label: B
choices.misconception: Chose a vague detail.

choices.label: C
choices.body: The butterfly rested on a milkweed leaf for ten minutes.

choices.label: D
choices.misconception: Chose a vague detail.

subject: language
stem: Which detail does not belong in a paragraph about recycling?
explanation: The cousin sentence is off topic.

choices.label: A
choices.misconception: Did not identify the unrelated detail.

choices.label: B
choices.body: Paper can be sorted into bins.
choices.misconception: Did not identify the unrelated detail.

choices.label: C
choices.body: Recycling keeps some trash out of landfills.
choices.misconception: Did not identify the unrelated detail.

choices.label: D
choices.body: My cousin plays soccer after school.

subject: language
stem: Which revision develops the idea best?
explanation: The best revision explains why the aquifer matters.

choices.label: A
choices.body: The aquifer matters because cities use its water during dry months.

choices.label: B
choices.misconception: Chose a vague or underdeveloped detail.

choices.label: C
choices.misconception: Chose a vague or underdeveloped detail.

choices.label: D
choices.body: The aquifer is a thing underground.
choices.misconception: Chose a vague or underdeveloped detail.

subject: language
stem: Which detail best supports an essay about safe biking?
explanation: The helmet detail directly supports the idea of safe biking.

choices.label: A
choices.misconception: Picked an interesting but weak or off-topic detail.

choices.label: B
choices.body: A helmet can protect a rider during a fall.

choices.label: C
choices.body: My bike is green and shiny.
choices.misconception: Picked an interesting but weak or off-topic detail.

choices.label: D
choices.misconception: Picked an interesting but weak or off-topic detail.

subject: language
stem: Which sentence uses right correctly?
explanation: Right can mean the opposite of left.

choices.label: A
choices.misconception: Chose a sentence where the homograph meaning does not fit.

choices.label: B
choices.misconception: Chose a sentence where the homograph meaning does not fit.

choices.label: D
choices.body: I will right my bike to school.
choices.misconception: Chose a sentence where the homograph meaning does not fit.

subject: language
stem: Choose the meaning of bat in: The bat flew at dusk.
explanation: At dusk and flew show bat means the animal.

choices.label: A
choices.misconception: Ignored context clues for a homograph.

choices.label: B
choices.misconception: Ignored context clues for a homograph.

choices.label: C
choices.misconception: Ignored context clues for a homograph.

subject: language
stem: Choose the correct word: Please ___ your name clearly.
explanation: Write means to form words.

choices.label: B
choices.misconception: Confused homophones.

choices.label: C
choices.misconception: Confused homophones.

choices.label: D
choices.misconception: Confused homophones.

subject: language
stem: Choose the meaning of present: The present speaker is Dr. Lee.
explanation: Present describes the speaker who is here now.

choices.label: A
choices.misconception: Used the wrong meaning of a homograph.

choices.label: C
choices.misconception: Used the wrong meaning of a homograph.

choices.label: D
choices.misconception: Used the wrong meaning of a homograph.

subject: language
stem: Which sentence uses close as an adjective?
explanation: Close describes distance, so it is an adjective here.

choices.label: A
choices.body: They close the store at six.
choices.misconception: Confused verb and adjective meanings of a homograph.

choices.label: B
choices.body: We will close the meeting now.
choices.misconception: Confused verb and adjective meanings of a homograph.

choices.label: C
choices.body: The store is close to my house.

choices.label: D
choices.misconception: Confused verb and adjective meanings of a homograph.

subject: language
stem: Which word is the best synonym for happy?
explanation: Joyful has a similar meaning to happy.

choices.label: A
choices.misconception: Chose an antonym or unrelated word.

choices.label: B
choices.misconception: Chose an antonym or unrelated word.

choices.label: C
choices.misconception: Chose an antonym or unrelated word.

subject: language
stem: Which word could replace tiny?
explanation: Small is a synonym for tiny.

choices.label: B
choices.misconception: Confused synonym with antonym or unrelated word.

choices.label: C
choices.misconception: Confused synonym with antonym or unrelated word.

choices.label: D
choices.misconception: Confused synonym with antonym or unrelated word.

subject: language
stem: A thesaurus would help you find ___.
explanation: A thesaurus lists synonyms and related words.

choices.label: A
choices.misconception: Confused a thesaurus with other reference tools.

choices.label: C
choices.body: the page number of a chapter
choices.misconception: Confused a thesaurus with other reference tools.

choices.label: D
choices.misconception: Confused a thesaurus with other reference tools.

subject: language
stem: Which word best replaces walked in: Hana walked quietly into the room?
explanation: Tiptoed gives a more exact quiet way of walking.

choices.label: A
choices.misconception: Chose a word that changes the meaning.

choices.label: B
choices.misconception: Chose a word that changes the meaning.

choices.label: D
choices.misconception: Chose a word that changes the meaning.

subject: language
stem: Which replacement keeps the meaning but improves precision? The storm was big.
explanation: Enormous is a more precise synonym for big.

choices.label: A
choices.misconception: Chose an antonym or nonsensical replacement.

choices.label: B
choices.misconception: Chose an antonym or nonsensical replacement.

choices.label: C
choices.misconception: Chose an antonym or nonsensical replacement.
